author: @MichaelSkinner-e9j
text:
The thing about graphene is making cheaper, lower quality carbon fiber, plastics, or even concrete perform as well as the higher grades, and the highest grade perform even better.

Besides filters and electrodes, I think it’s really about improving and making the higher end cheaper and perform better, at least as cheaply as steel, without all the power and pollution, or actually Cannibalize the pollution to create something just as ubiquitous.

That’s really what’s holding graphene right now- cost and scale.  And it’s meant to make every day stuff we use scale even better.

I’m with you all the way on practicality!

author: @jamesmooney8933
text:
First of all,  you have imagine a sheet of graphene 18" x 36".  (I prefer base 12, to base 10 measurements, because you can't divide base 10 without going to infinity, but I digress).
At one atom thickness,  the idea of picking up the sheet would be impossible.   
A coat of oil would be thicker then a sheet of graphene.  Also would the graphene stick to its self like  Glad Wrap.
I believe that graphene will be part of a laminate,  possibly a coating on copper.

author: @haroldwestrich3312
text: I watched your video on making graphene and then during this video I came up with another theory for producing larger sheets. In effect - instead of trying to make a large sheet (like everyone I have seen so far) could long thin strips be made on the edge of copper plates (instead of the large flat surface) then moved to the side for the next thin strip to be "Grown" from the edge of the first strip. I know it sounds crazy difficult but it would be a continuous growth process rather than a huge batch. If people are currently able to make pieces of X width then they should be able to make long strips half that wide and possibly grow the new pieces close enough to the last piece that they could connect them with Silver Nano wires if nothing else YOU seem far more connected and capable of doing these experiments than I will be fore many years. I don't even have a workshop right now. Can you comment on why this theory would or would not work? I know you are busy but my email is oct1113@hotmail.com if you think the idea has any merit. Thank you for your videos

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not a question of what i think of the idea mate - i have no idea what is a good idea or not - i think what i think and other people differ in their views - i am ok with that but it also means i can't judge other folks ideas - unfortunately i also have no time to take on additional projects I am afraid if you want this idea explored you are going to have to explore it for yourself

author: @Warlitzor
text:
Well if you haven't really been hearing about graphene; or anything for that matter; as much as the next guy or essentially live under a rock *has little to no idea what the hype's supposed to be in regards to graphene's armament potential*, the simplistic application of the thing in question actually sounds more amazing than the hype, when thinking practically.

Might just be that I'm more concerned with energy portability (how we can make large energy storage the equivalent of storing 8 or more GB of space on jump drive) than the armament of the century.

But inasmuch as there's a lot of talk and (I guess) demonstrations of nanotech's capacity potentials, I've not come across anything that sounds grand scale.

Probably laziness and procrastination. ._.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Warlitzor or maybe the difficulty in translating experiment to real world application - my guess is that's more likely - but i am only guessing

replies.author: @Warlitzor
replies.text:
Well it varies on the thing in question, though of course it'd have to be done as well as possible before it sees general (civic and military) use, that much I know.

Though I've come across at least one civic use for graphene in regards to its conductive properties (relatively quick portable charger for iDevices), so that confirms what I was looking for in regards to the whole portable energy thing; small scale (haha, small, cuz it's a small charger th--Ok, I'll stop), but it's something.

Just a question of how far it can be taken as far as conductivity goes where it's at now and whether it'd be enough for high powered stuff. (I'ma be pessimistic and say no for now. |: ) And even that much I don't know about graphene: how it's so good at being a conductor and whether or not its conductivity can be stretched further.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Warlitzor i don't know the answer mate - i'm a practical guy so i would just try it and see - the problem there is i don't the answer to theoretical questions like that

replies.author: @Warlitzor
replies.text: Fair enough.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Warlitzor sorry mate

author: @incognittum
text: I would not use the word theory -- as you seem to refer to it -- and level it to speculations. We are inclined to make predictions and imagine a world unlike the current practical one we live in. Such a quality helps us orient our focus including the construction of theories which are not mere suppositions but based on a collection of facts, experiments, and educated gueses among other things that support the science behind the assumptions. I don't have a problem with your message apart from the fact that you're misleading people by leveling scientific theory with mere suppositions that you synonomously refer to as "theory".

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: fair enough - we all believe what we want to mate

replies.author: @incognittum
replies.text: I don't believe it's fair enough. I am not arguing against your oppinion but rather against your use of the word "theory". Consequently, seeing as you are striving for truth and likewise improvement, don't you think it's important to communicate effectively your ideas? I believe it's important when you're presenting your views against currently existing notions; such is a theory as a well defined concept unlike the way in which you use it. Likewise, there is not such thing as "a little truth". That's merely my argument against your word choice and not against your thoughs on the subject matter of your video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: incognittum I don't strive for truth and arguments about words are - to my mind - a waste of time. A message is an interplay between giver and receiver - there is no exact in the way I see things - but hey ho - we all believe what we want lol

replies.author: @user-md9fg7cz6q
replies.text: @ incognittum, Do you realize that you just told him not to call "scientific theory", "theory" ? L...O...L.  So would my supposition, that you regularly kick back with a smug sense of accomplishment, whilst starring at a framed document that you paid way to much for?  Also would the speculation that you maybe make a living by deriving theories from others actual experimentation ?  Now are these suppositions, theory, conjecture, just wild guesses, because with the current state of things, I wager I could get peer reviewed on less.  Truly, since theories can't be duplicated, could I be published ?

author: @lisasis2c235
text: Your encouragement for the physical practice with the material with a focus on solving problems is exactly what's needed for people to start taking charge of their lives and our communities.  There are a few problems I want to solve and although its been decades since I worked in a lab, listening and watching you is just the grease to get my own elbows lifting a few books and playing with some new material.  Any chance you can offer a suggestion about looking for information about creating a filter for purifying water?  My first intuition is to apply one of your paints to a coffee filter and measure the purity of the water.  If you have any resources or suggestions I'd be most grateful and of course I'm sure their world will be a safer place for everyone.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: as a technique hydrothermal carbonisation (HTC) is going to serve you well - we all know activated carbon is a good filter - you could look at carbonising already existing carbon structures using hydrothermal carbonisation then activating them and seeing what happens - for a start you could HTC the filter paper

author: @lorddorker3703
text: I think I've found DNA based graphine. Using the chemical process pyrolysis to make biochar from plants I've come across electrically conductive biochar. The higher the temperature the lower the electrical resistance, Chlorophyll is supposed to be able to react at the single photon level. I speculate that when all the other components are 'cooked' off and the carbon is left behind, some of that carbon is forming graphine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hmm - maybe. but I strongly suspect what you are getting is nitrogen doped carbon structures - this is good stuff for capacitors

author: @CoyoteGenius314
text: Interesting! I'm no chemist but carbon is a gas and in chemistry for it to be a solid at atmospheric temperature it would have to be bonded with some element. In the 1800's it was said that phosphorus has a great affinity for carbon and releases energy in combination. Like wise it takes much energy in their separation. So if you had a layer of graphite (carbon/phosphorus bonded) and layer of more phosphorus and applied enough voltage it may form sheet. Or isolated from atmosphere with dielectric in between layers and you may have a charged capacitor battery. Phosphorus is said to glow in presence of O2 but could it be CO2?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: carbon has lots of different physical  forms - they are called allotropes. One allotrope of carbon is diamond, another  is coal - they are pure carbon - just different physical forms

replies.author: @CoyoteGenius314
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I understand modern science tells us it's pure but there are many things from years ago that have been removed from teachings because they don't want us to know. Has it ever been checked for presence of phosphorus? H2O even distilled you have a pH level. Were is the "p" from? If phosphorus were proved to be a present bonding element science would have to be rewritten. Possibilities could be endless. Conductivity of el wire phosphorescence is like carbon.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
CoyoteGenius314
Yes it has and there is no phosphorus. I think your basic idea of layering up phospherus and carbon has merit but you need to check a few things out first the p in pH stands for parts - pH is a measure of the number of hydrogen ions - as for what bonds elements together - well nobody really knows - they guess at bonding models and those are the ones we use - it used to be called phlogiston - i think - but i would have to check that - now they are going for quantum energies - but one thing it probably isn't is another element - It's good to question things but you need to question from a background - have a read around at some basic concepts and see what is being said then how does that differ from what you think. You can't just question for the sake of questioning - that leads nowhere

replies.author: @CoyoteGenius314

author: @whig01
text: Tesla's point wasn't that we could build a giant homopolar generator the size of the earth. He was modeling the earth itself as an actual homopolar generator in fact.

author: @algirdaslazauskas2733
text: The method you used to mechanically polish the graphite powder on the substrate is the right one. Afterwards, you process that graphite coated substrate in specific ultrasonic bath or in acoustic emission chamber combined with vibration to delaminate the layers of unwanted graphite. As a result, one or several layers thick graphene sheets on the substrate are obtained. Once you done that, you will need to fill up the space between graphene sheets (i.e. to connect them together). The procedure is done via epitaxial chemical vapor deposition. Afterwards, a sheet of graphene on substrate in quadratic meters is obtained. No patents are available as a single person or a group of persons would not manage to perform the describe technological steps in such a scale. The technology exists, I have seen it…

author: @Aerros
text:
The idea here is all about balance:
 
You can either start with theory or start with practice.
	If you start with theory (ideas/thoughts), these will eventually have to be experimented, then refined again with theory. 
	If you start with practice (experimentation), you're going to have to refine what you observed with theory. 

Too much theory makes a day dreamer. 
Too much experimentation makes a brute.

author: @m3sca1
text:
i liken these sheets of graphene to ice sheets...they are randomly shaped
and what i saw in the pressure cooker after multiple heating cycles...the stuff started to grow and if it broke from the pot being moved, after some time the sheets seemed to heal and coalesce together again.
it doesn't fully heal though and you can see gaps.
running another heat cycle gathered more graphene to push up from the bottom.
am planning a run in the pressure cooker that has a small table with a substrate sitting on the table.
as the water evaporates ,the plan is to let the water level drop leaving the graphene sheet high and dry on the table.
will keep you posted.

a graphene based ballistic armour would probably work well with the graphene sheets edge on to the incoming bullet.
like trying to shoot thru a phone book edge...the paper catches up on itself and bunches up and absorbs the impact.

i wonder if you could titrate the amount of graphene to be added to an ink base by measuring the resistance as the graphene oxide is added and stop when the Ohms reading starts to rise... 

try blu taccing a foam ear plug over the mic input of your new camera mate.
sounds like a very sensitive electret picking up too much backgournd noise.
i had a camera that needed some damping on the microphone port once.
